front: Poem: epic about the Civil War that begins with an invocation to the 'American muse,' described as 'the buffalo-ghost, the broncho-ghost / with silver dollar in your saddle-horn'
Poem: Sally Dupre thinks 'Wingate Hall must tumble down' while dancing with a plantation owner's son in this Civil War epic
Poem: narrated by Connecticut abolitionist Jack Ellyat; title derives from a song whose subject's 'body lies a-mouldering in the grave'
Short Story: a jury including Blackbeard and King Philip (Metacom) voids a contract in which a farmer sold his soul to Mr. Scratch
back: The Devil and Daniel Webster (Stephen Vincent Benét)

front: Short Story: the protagonist gets startled when he hears Miser Stevens' voice coming from Mr. Scratch's handkerchief
back: The Devil and Daniel Webster (Stephen Vincent Benét)

front: Poem: the speaker declares 'I have fallen in love with' the title objects, praising Deadwood, Lost Mule Flat, Little French Lick, and Medicine Hat
Poem: ends with the line 'Bury my heart at Wounded Knee,' which provided the title for a Dee Brown book
Short Story: a priest's son named John journeys to the 'Place of the Gods,' which is revealed to be New York City after 'The Great Burning'
back: By the Waters of Babylon (Stephen Vincent Benét)

front: Short Story: the protagonist encounters a column reading 'ASHING' (George Washington) and realizes the gods were men; concludes 'we must build again'
back: By the Waters of Babylon (Stephen Vincent Benét)

front: Short Story: title from Psalm 137; the protagonist decides to travel east after shooting an arrow through a panther's eye
back: By the Waters of Babylon (Stephen Vincent Benét)

front: Poem: unfinished narrative poem about the settling of the American frontier; won a posthumous Pulitzer in 1944
Short Story: Monsieur Tibault becomes the finest conductor in Europe despite conducting with his tail
back: The King of the Cats (Stephen Vincent Benét)
